content.problemStatement:
Chef has nnn meeting rooms totalRoomstotalRoomstotalRooms numbered from 000 to n−1n-1n−1 . Chef wants to schedule a series of meetings meetingSchedulesmeetingSchedulesmeetingSchedules, each with a unique start time and a defined end time. 

Every meeting must be assigned to the unused room with the lowest number available at its start time. If no rooms are free, Chef delays the meeting until a room becomes available, keeping the original meeting duration intact. 

When multiple meetings are waiting, Chef prioritizes those with earlier original start times to occupy newly freed rooms.

Help Chef determine which room is booked the most times after scheduling all meetings. If multiple rooms share the highest booking count, Chef wants to know the smallest room number among them.

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindMostBookedRoomfindMostBookedRoomfindMostBookedRoom

Parameters
totalRoomstotalRoomstotalRooms : The total number of available meeting rooms, numbered from 000 to totalRooms−1totalRooms - 1totalRooms−1.
meetingSchedulesmeetingSchedulesmeetingSchedules : A list where each element is a vector [start,end][start, end][start,end] representing the start and end times of a meeting. All start times are unique.
Return Value
Returns an integer representing the room number that is booked the most times.
If multiple rooms have the same highest booking count, returns the smallest room number among them.
Constraints
1≤T≤101 \leq T \leq 101≤T≤10
1≤n≤1001 \leq n \leq 1001≤n≤100
1≤number of meetings≤1051 \leq \text{number of meetings} \leq 10^51≤number of meetings≤105
0≤starti<endi≤5×1050 \leq start_i < end_i \leq 5 \times 10^50≤starti​<endi​≤5×105
All startistart_istarti​ are unique across each test case.

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains an integer nnn — the number of meeting rooms.
The following mmm lines each contain two integers startistart_istarti​ and endiend_iendi​ — the start and end times of the ithi^{th}ith meeting.




Output Format

For each test case, print a single integer — the room number that Chef finds to be booked the most times after scheduling all meetings according to the rules.
constraints: All start_i are unique across each test case
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains an integer n, the number of meeting rooms, followed by m lines, each containing two integers start and end, representing the start and end times of a meeting.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the room number that is booked the most times after scheduling all meetings according to the rules.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Test case 1: All of the other meeting can be merged into the first one [1,20]. Test case 2: Three meetings [0,5], [2,7], and [4,6] all overlap, requiring zero free rooms at any time.

Explanation: All meetings are consecutive without overlap: [1-2], [2-3], [3-4], [4-5]. One room is enough since each meeting starts exactly when the previous ends. Thus, the output is 0 (no additional rooms needed beyond the first).
Explanation: Only one meeting and one room, so the meeting is assigned to the only room.

solution.approach: The key insight is to use a priority queue to keep track of the meeting rooms that are available and the meetings that are waiting to be assigned to a room. We iterate through the meetings in order of their start times. For each meeting, we check if there are any available rooms. If there are, we assign the meeting to the room with the lowest number. If there are no available rooms, we delay the meeting until a room becomes available. We use a priority queue to keep track of the rooms that are available and the meetings that are waiting to be assigned to a room.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * m * log n) per test case, where m is the number of meetings and n is the number of rooms. The total time complexity is O(T * m * log n) since we iterate through all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n + m) — we use a priority queue to keep track of the available rooms and the meetings that are waiting to be assigned to a room.

code.Python3:
# COMPLETE, RUNNABLE Python3 code
# Uses input() for lines
# Handles all T test cases
import sys
import heapq

def findMostBookedRoom(totalRooms, meetings):
    freeRooms = list(range(totalRooms))
    heapq.heapify(freeRooms)

    busyRooms = []

    roomCount = [0] * totalRooms

    for start, end in meetings:
        duration = end - start

        # Free rooms that are available by start time
        while busyRooms and busyRooms[0][0] <= start:
            heapq.heappop(busyRooms)
            heapq.heappush(freeRooms, busyRooms[0][1])

        if freeRooms:
            room = heapq.heappop(freeRooms)
            heapq.heappush(busyRooms, (end, room))
            roomCount[room] += 1
        else:
            freeTime, room = heapq.heappop(busyRooms)
            heapq.heappush(busyRooms, (freeTime + duration, room))
            roomCount[room] += 1

    maxRoom = 0
    for i in range(1, totalRooms):
        if roomCount[i] > roomCount[maxRoom]:
            maxRoom = i

    return maxRoom

T = int(input())
for t in range(T):
    totalRooms = int(input())
    mSize = int(input())
    meetings = [list(map(int, input().split())) for _ in range(mSize)]
    print(findMostBookedRoom(totalRooms, meetings))
